tipRules.tip: Hackney: 'Fish in the southern part of the country are still in that blah summer mode where they don't really want to bite a bigger bait. That's why I love using this smaller jig because it's something these fish don't really see.'
Hackney: 'Once fall fully gets here, that's when I like to increase the size of my jigs.' Use the fluorocarbon flipping jig in early fall as a transition bait before upsizing.

Hackney: 'Even though it's smaller, you can still fish it on heavy tackle and fish it like you would a bigger and heavier jig. I like to use 20- or 25-pound fluorocarbon or 50-pound braid depending on the cover.'
Hackney: 'When I'm working in grass I go lighter (fluorocarbon), but with the brush I move up to braid.'

knowledge.insight: Hackney: 'The end of summer is one of the most difficult times to fish in the South. Downsizing to a compact finesse jig gives pressured bass something they haven't seen.'
Hackney: 'The zero-degree line tie has the same effect as a Texas rig — the line goes straight down making it weedless. Perfect for going through brush and cover without getting stuck.'
Hackney: 'Usually when you get stuck with a jig, it's not because the weed guard is giving, it's because the guard is too far away from the hook point. That's how brush can get up under there.'
Hackney: 'The thinner skirt gives a sleeker presentation for finicky fish. It's also great for smallmouth and spotted bass with their smaller mouths.'
